name: MacTutor
biography:
Louis Lef횄짤bure de Fourcy was born in Port-au-Prince, a city laid out by the French in 1749 when it was named L'H횄쨈pital. In 1770 it became the capital of the French colony of Saint-Domingue. His father, 횄혟tienne-Louis Lefebvre, called himself Lefebvre du Coudray but, when he married in Saint-Domingue, it was under the name 횄혟tienne-Louis Lefebvre de Fourcy. The French colony of Saint-Domingue was, by the middle of the 18 th century, the most prosperous of France's possessions. Its wealth came from plantation agriculture and it was in this business that 횄혟tienne-Louis worked. The environmental damage caused by clearing trees for plantations led to marked drops in productivity and this, together with political problems from a growing independence movement, meant that 횄혟tienne-Louis had hit hard times by the time his son was born. His son was given the name Louis Lef횄짤bure de Fourcy and this version appears on surviving baptismal records. The child only spent the first couple of years of his life in Saint-Domingue for, by 1789 , his mother had brought him to France. His father remained in Saint-Domingue where rebellions against French rule broke out in 1891 . 횄혟tienne-Louis Lefebvre died in Saint-Domingue in 1794 .
Louis Lef횄짤bure de Fourcy spent his childhood in Nantes but was sent to Paris to complete his education at the Coll횄짢ge de la Marine et des Colonies. From there he won a scholarship to study at the 횄혟cole Polytechnique which he entered in 1803 at the age of sixteen. Among those who entered in the same year, we mention Fran횄짠ois Arago , Charles-Julien Brianchon and Claude-Louis Mathieu . These were the early years of the 횄혟cole Polytechnique during which it operated out of buildings attached to the Palais Bourbon, where the French parliament met. The three years that Lef횄짤bure de Fourcy spent at the 횄혟cole Polytechnique were, of course, years during which France was in the grip of the French Revolution. One consequence of this was that Lef횄짤bure, as we shall name him for the remainder of this biography, dropped the aristocratic "de Fourcy" in his name. Rather more surprising, however, is that he registered under his father's name so during his studies he was known as 횄혟tienne-Louis Lefebvre. France had adopted the revolutionary calendar so Lef횄짤bure and his fellow students studied for eight and a half days in each 10 day week at the 횄혟cole Polytechnique. Ivor Grattan-Guinness writes [ 3 ] :- The dominant role of mathematics soon led the students to deploy the catchword 'X' as a name for the school. Note that Roland Brasseur refers to the 횄혟cole Polytechnique as X throughout the article [ 2 ] . Lef횄짤bure graduated from the 횄혟cole Polytechnique in 1805 and enrolled in the 횄혟cole d'artillerie in Metz. However, he left almost immediately and was appointed to the Pont-Levoy College in Loir-et-Cher. His circumstances had changed in January 1806 which may have been the reason for the change in his plans. After the death of his father in 1794 , his mother had remarried and had two sons before separating from her new husband. She died in January 1806 and this left Lef횄짤bure responsible for bringing up his two half-brothers. By May 1807 he was back in Paris at the 횄혟cole Polytechnique where he was appointed as an assistant r횄짤p횄짤titeur in analysis. Things had changed since he was a student. Napoleon Bonapart was now emperor of France and he had given the 횄혟cole Polytechnique a more military look. It had also moved from the buildings it had occupied when Lef횄짤bure was a student, to occupy two colleges not far from the Panth횄짤on. He was awarded a doctorate in 1811 after submitting two theses. One, on mechanics, 횄혟quations g횄짤n횄짤rales du mouvement des fluides et application de ces 횄짤quations 횄혻 la th횄짤orie du son 창혫혟 ( General equations of fluid motion and application of these equations to the theory of sound ) consisted of five pages, while the second, on astronomy, De l'attraction des sph횄짤ro횄짱des et de la figure des plan횄짢tes 창혫혟 ( On the attraction of spheroids and the shape of planets ) , contained eleven pages. He dedicated these works to Sylvestre Lacroix who must have acted as an advisor.
He became a r횄짤p횄짤titeur in analysis in 1813 and in applied analysis in the following year. Around this time there were major changes in France with the defeat of Napoleon in 1814 and the restoration of the monarchy. As a result of these changes, Lef횄짤bure stopped using his father's name and went back to signing himself Louis Lef횄짤bure de Fourcy. He became a r횄짤p횄짤titeur in descriptive geometry in 1815 and, from 1817 to 1820 , he was an examiner in descriptive geometry and the 'graphic arts'. In addition to his duties at the 횄혟cole Polytechnique, Lef횄짤bure was appointed to teach mathematics at the College Louis-le-Grand in December 1814 . He had married Marie Fran횄짠oise Victoire Peyron and they had two sons, Michel-Eugene Lef횄짤bure de Fourcy born in November 1812 and Charles Lef횄짤bure de Fourcy born in 1815 . Michel-Eugene became an engineer and married three times. His first wife was Anne Lassale (1809 - 1844) , his second wife Victoire-Ana횄짱s Mousset (1824 - 1847) , and his third wife being Marie St횄짤phanie Lam횄짤 (1824 - 1909) , Gabriel Lam횄짤 's daughter. Charles Lef횄짤bure de Fourcy became Inspector General of Roads and Bridges and participated in the building of the Suez Canal. He married Nathalie Hennequin (1822 - 1903) .
At Louis-le-Grand, Louis Lef횄짤bure de Fourcy was promoted to teach the special class in October 1817 . Of the 21 students in the special class of 1817 - 18 , one was the poet, novelist, and dramatist, Victor Hugo. Many years later, he spoke about his mathematics teacher:- Professor M Lefebure de Fourcy, lanky with shoulders which incongruously supported a long pockmarked face, had energy and wit. He crossed the class in a stride, giving rapid demonstrations, streaking the table with lines. The special mathematics class was held every afternoon, Monday to Saturday. As to its content, Victor Hugo explained that the course did not have:- ... a detailed plan, but a list of books by which it is taught, and the authors for the mathematics texts are Euler , Lagrange and Lacroix in algebra, Lacroix or Biot for applications of algebra to geometry, Monge and Poinsot for statics, and Delambre or Biot for astronomy. The Royal College of Saint-Louis was founded in 1820 and Lef횄짤bure was appointed as professor of mathematics. There he taught the special class and among his pupils we mention in particular Joseph Liouville . Lef횄짤bure taught at the College of Saint-Louis for five years, leaving in December 1825 when he was appointed as an assistant to Sylvestre Lacroix at the faculty of Science in Paris. This led to his appointment as admissions examiner for the 횄혟cole Polytechnique in 1826 . Ivor Grattan-Guinness explains [ 3 ] :- Students were chosen by admissions examiners. The country was divided into four regions, and also Paris, and an examiner travelled in a region for some weeks during the summer to interview promising young boys nominated by local families or dignitaries. The examiners and some school officers then met and chose the enrolment for the following autumn. The 횄혟cole Polytechnique declared that being an admissions examiner was incompatible with teaching, so Lef횄짤bure had free time which he devoted to writing student textbooks. He began publishing a series of books in 1827 and over the next few years various texts appeared: Le횄짠ons de g횄짤om횄짤trie analytique 창혫혟 ( Lessons in analytic geometry ) (1827) , Th횄짤ories du plus grand commun diviseur alg횄짤brique et de l'횄짤limination entre deux 횄짤quations 횄혻 deux inconnue 창혫혟 ( Theories of the algebraic greatest common divisor  and the elimination between two equations with two unknowns ) (1827) , Trait횄짤 de g횄짤om횄짤trie descriptive 창혫혟 ( Treatise on descriptive geometry ) (2 vols. 1829 - 1830) , 횄혟l횄짤ments de trigonom횄짤trie 창혫혟 ( Elements of trigonometry ) (1830) , Le횄짠ons d'alg횄짢bre 창혫혟 ( Algebra lessons ) (1833) .
The title page of Le횄짠ons de g횄짤om횄짤trie analytique 창혫혟 ( Analytic geometry lessons ) (1827) lists Lef횄짤bure as:- Chevalier de la L횄짤gion-d'Honneur, Examinateur des Aspirans 횄혻 l'횄혟cole Royale Polytechnique, 횄혻 l'횄혟cole Sp횄짤ciale Militaire, 횄혻 l'횄혟cole de Marine et 횄혻 l'횄혟cole Foresti횄짢re. 창혫혟 ( Chevalier of the Legion of Honor, Examiner of midshipmen at the Royal Polytechni  School, the Military Academy and the School of Marine and Forestry School. ) This book contained a version of the lectures that Lef횄짤bure had given at the Royal College of Saint-Louis while he occupied the chair of Special Mathematics. The popularity of this book is indicated by the fact that by 1881 a tenth edition had been produced. Trait횄짤 de g횄짤om횄짤trie descriptive 창혫혟 ( Treatise on descriptive geometry ) was essentially translated into English by Thomas Grainger Hall, of King's College, London, when he wrote his book The elements of descriptive geometry (1841) . In the Preface, Hall explains the position of descriptive geometry:- Invented by the genius of Monge , and pursued with ardour and success by the most eminent French geometricians, it is now taught in almost all universities and in the principal schools of the continent. In England it was unknown, as a branch of instruction, until lectures were given upon it by Mr Bradley , in the Engineering Department of King's College; and the present work has been undertaken to supply the students with a text book, that by it they might the more profitably attend to what they heard in the lecture room: and as an elementary book was necessary for beginners, it has been thought expedient to place before the students, in an English dress, one which has stood the test of experience. The treatise on Descriptive geometry, by Mr Lef횄짤bure de Fourcy has, therefore, been selected, and the following pages are, for the most part, translated from it. Lef횄짤bure's text was very popular and in 1881 the eighth edition of the book was published. By 1858 a seventh edition of 횄혟l횄짤ments de trigonom횄짤trie 창혫혟 ( Elements of trigonometry ) was in print while Le횄짠ons d'alg횄짢bre 창혫혟 ( Algebra lessons ) , much revised over the years, was in its tenth edition in 1893 . In the Preface to the original 1833 edition of Le횄짠ons d'alg횄짢bre 창혫혟 ( Algebra lessons ) , Lef횄짤bure states:- If I had limited the contents to that which is required for admission to the 횄혟cole Polytechnique, it would have formed a much less extensive volume. Lef횄짤bure's books are praised by his son in [ 1 ] as having:- ... good ordering of materials, elegant methods, and clarity of style. However, other have criticised them for being "totally devoid of original ideas." It would be tempting to believe that their popularity might have come from the fact that many students trying to gain entry to top education institutions had to be examined by Lef횄짤bure. However, the popularity of these texts continued long after Lef횄짤bure ceased being an examiner.
As an examiner, Lef횄짤bure had a reputation which made the candidates extremely fearful. Together with Louis Benjamin Francoeur and Jean Nicolas Pierre Hachette , Lef횄짤bure examined 횄혟variste Galois for his baccalaureate on 14 December 1828 . By two votes to one, the decision of the examiners was that Galois should not enter the 횄혟cole Polytechnique. Most students dreaded being examined by Lef횄짤bure and a report by an Inspector from the Paris Academy of Sciences lets us see the reason:- Several times I attended oral examinations by Mr Lef횄짤bure de Fourcy and this is my impression. The professor cannot be considered a benevolent reviewer, his voice is harsh, his tone is dismissive, also he questioned in a very firm stiff manner, and showed no mercy for any errors of logic. It was not only with students that Lef횄짤bure was severe and demanding, but he behaved in the same way towards his own sons. He prepared his son Michel-Eugene for the entrance examinations of the 횄혟cole Polytechnique. Michel-Eugene wrote [ 1 ] :- Twice a week, my father brought us together, one of my friends and I, in front of his old blackboard. I dreaded like fire these sessions, sometimes stormy bringing me to tears. If fear is the beginning of wisdom, perhaps that was how I entered the 횄혟cole Polytechnique at the first examination [ in 1829] . In May 1843 Lacroix died and, in July of the same year, Lef횄짤bure succeeded him in the chair of differential and integral calculus at the Faculty of Science in Paris. He gave up his role of admissions examiner at the 횄혟cole Polytechnique at this time. However, he did not distinguish himself as a higher education lecturer. A report by an Inspector from the Paris Academy of Sciences , written in 1860 , states that:- ... his teaching is of a low standard, and has no originality. The Professor simply repeats what he has learned previously. [ Lef횄짤bure, who ] acquired a good reputation in secondary education, is far from successful, however, in higher education. It was around this time that his health began to fail and Lef횄짤bure retired from the Faculty of Science in September 1863 . His wife died in December of the following year after a painful illness throughout which, his son wrote, Lef횄짤bure suffered in silence. He died on 12 March 1869 and his funeral was held three days later at the Church of Saint-Sulphice. At the Montparnasse Cemetery an address was given by Joseph Serret .
